mappings.rationale: Directly covers Judaism including its history, Torah, Talmud, prayer, festivals, and present-day forms (Orthodox, Reform, Conservative), the primary subject of 826.
Directly covers the TaNaKh/Old Testament including Deuteronomy, Exodus, Psalms, and the Ten Commandments, relevant to 826.B.1.b.
Examines the Hebrew Bible as sacred literature including David, Jacob, poetry, and Song of Songs, relevant to 826.B.1.
Addresses the Bible's Abrahamic context and the history of biblical interpretation, with attention to the Hebrew scriptures, relevant to 826.B.1.
Directly covers the Qumran literature (Dead Sea Scrolls), the specific subject of 826.B.2.
Traces the history of the Jewish people from the Ark of the Covenant and the Exodus through exile, assimilation, and emancipation, relevant to 826.A.
Surveys Jewish literary traditions across languages and diaspora communities, relevant to 826.B on intellectual and imaginative expressions.
Directly covers Jewish mysticism (Kabbalah) including the Zohar, Names of God, and Hasidism, relevant to 826.B.5 on mystical and devotional writings.
Presents Maimonides as a foundational Jewish philosopher, examining his Mishneh Torah, Guide for the Perplexed, and rationalist methodology, relevant to 826.B.6 on Jewish philosophical writings.
Treats Maimonides, Gersonides, and other Jewish thinkers alongside Christian and Islamic philosophy, relevant to 826.B.6 on Jewish philosophical writings.
Presents archaeological evidence for biblical history and the land of Israel, including Dead Sea Scroll discoveries, relevant to 826.A.1 and 826.C.2.d on sacred places.
Traces Zionism as a political and cultural movement tied to Jewish identity, the Hebrew language, and the land of Israel, relevant to 826.A.5 and 826.C.2.d.
Examines the Israeli-Palestinian conflict in relation to Jewish religious claims to Jerusalem and the land of Israel, relevant to 826.C.2.d.
Documents the history of antisemitism and its devastating impact on Jewish communities across the medieval and modern eras, relevant to 826.A.4-5.
Traces the history of the Jewish ghetto from Venice to Eastern Europe and its effects on communal life and religious practice, relevant to 826.A.4 on the medieval era.
The Holocaust profoundly impacted Judaism and Jewish communities, relevant to 826.A.5 on developments in modern Judaism.
Explores diaspora communities with particular attention to the Jewish diaspora's formation after the destruction of the Second Temple, relevant to 826.A.6.
Examines concepts of God including Jewish monotheism and the covenant relationship between God and Israel, relevant to 826.C.1.a on doctrines concerning God.
Places Judaism in comparative perspective with Christianity and Islam, illuminating its foundational role among the Abrahamic traditions.
Paul's theology on Jewish law, Torah observance, and salvation emerges directly from the Hellenistic Jewish context, relevant to 826.A.2 on the Hellenistic era.
The Enlightenment, particularly through Moses Mendelssohn's Haskalah, shaped Jewish emancipation and Reform Judaism, relevant to 826.A.5 and 826.C.3.b.
Introduces interpretive theory relevant to Judaic methods of scriptural exegesis and hermeneutics (826.B.4).
Explores ritual practices relevant to Jewish sabbath observance, holiday celebrations, and rites of passage such as bar mitzvah, relevant to 826.C.2.a-c.
The Crusades brought devastating persecution and pogroms against Jewish communities across medieval Europe, relevant to 826.A.4 on the medieval era.
Surveys Judaism in the American context, shedding light on modern denominational forms (Reform, Conservative, Reconstructionist) discussed in 826.C.3.
